narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 45.2% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 31, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles of 3 and 2 respectively, middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27 and Year 3 deciles average 5.91, giving context to how the model weighed your profile. The three biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 grade decile, and your LSAT score. Your solid undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward a higher chance of passing the bar, as it stands above the 3.24 average for successful students. In contrast, your Year 3 decile had the strongest negative pull, significantly increasing the model's concern about bar failure since it ranks lower than typical passers. Your LSAT score also leaned against you, though less forcefully, contributing to the overall risk assessment by falling short of what the model sees in most who succeed. Being full-time aligns well with passers, where 93% go that route, and your middle income fits a common profile among them at 35%, but these didn't outweigh the key academic signals. Compared to typical accepted students, your profile shows strengths in undergrad performance but gaps in later grades and test scores that tipped the balance. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model's data-driven read on bar success patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted prep, and reapplying with an updated profile that addresses these areas. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by focusing on these steps, and we're here to support your next application.
